title: Writing Essays When Your Brain Outraces Your Fingers
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have ideas. Strong ones. They arrive in a rush, fully formed, connected to each other in ways that feel obvious and exciting. You sit down to write. And then everything falls apart. Your fingers cannot keep up with your brain. By the time you finish typing one sentence, the next three ideas have evaporated. The words on the page do not match what you intended. The spelling is off. The sentences trail into fragments. The paragraph that felt so clear in your head reads like chaos on the screen. You delete it. You start over. An hour passes, and you have a single paragraph you hate. This is the experience of writing with ADHD and dyslexia. Your brain generates ideas faster than your output systems can capture them. The bottleneck is not thinking. The bottleneck is getting the thoughts out. Here is how to build a writing workflow that fits your brain.

Separate Thinking From Writing

The standard writing process assumes you can think and write simultaneously. You sit down, open a document, and produce a draft that combines idea generation with sentence construction. For the ADHD-dyslexic brain, this is a recipe for frustration. The two processes interfere with each other. Thinking generates ideas rapidly. Writing slows everything down. The mismatch creates a traffic jam in your working memory.

The solution is to separate the two completely. Do not write your essay as a first step. Speak it. Use a voice memo app on your phone. Record yourself explaining your argument out loud, as if you were describing it to a friend. Do not worry about structure, grammar, or academic tone. Just talk. Follow the ideas wherever they lead. The recording is not your essay. It is raw material for your essay.

When you listen back to the recording, you will hear your argument. It will be disorganized. It will include tangents and repetitions. But the core ideas will be there, and they will be in your authentic voice. Transcribe the best parts. Now you have text on the page that came from your mouth rather than your fingers. The blank document problem is solved. You are no longer writing from scratch. You are editing something that already exists.

Use Dictation for Drafting

Once you have your ideas outlined from your voice memo, use dictation software to write your actual draft. Most computers and phones have built-in dictation. Dragon NaturallySpeaking is more powerful and accurate. Google Docs has voice typing. Speak your paragraphs. Watch the words appear on screen.

Dictation bypasses multiple barriers. It removes the handwriting or typing bottleneck. It eliminates the spelling errors that interrupt your flow and make you self-conscious. It captures your ideas at the speed of speech, which is closer to the speed of thought than typing will ever be. The dictation will not be perfect. You will need to go back and fix punctuation, homophones, and words the software misheard. But fixing a flawed draft is easier than producing a perfect draft from nothing.

If dictation feels awkward at first, that is normal. Speaking your thoughts to a machine is a learned skill. Start with short sessions. Dictate one paragraph. Edit it. Dictate the next. The more you practice, the more natural it becomes. Many students find that after a few weeks, dictation is faster and less exhausting than typing.

Build a Scaffolding Before You Write

ADHD brains struggle with open-ended tasks. A blank document is an open-ended task. Dyslexic brains struggle with organizing written language. Without a structure, sentences wander. Before you write anything, build a detailed scaffold. This is more than an outline. A scaffold tells you exactly what goes where.

For each paragraph, write a single sentence that states the main point. Under that sentence, list the evidence you will use. A quote. A statistic. A specific example. Under each piece of evidence, write a phrase explaining how it connects to your argument. This is not your essay. It is the frame on which your essay will hang. When you begin to write, you are not deciding what to say. You are filling in the details of a structure that already exists. The decisions have been made. The writing is just execution.

This scaffolding process plays to ADHD strengths. Breaking a large task into small, specific pieces is exactly what the ADHD brain needs to overcome initiation paralysis. Each scaffold component is a micro-task with a clear endpoint. Write the topic sentence for paragraph one. Done. List the evidence for paragraph one. Done. The scaffold builds momentum. By the time you begin drafting, you have already completed a dozen small tasks. The essay feels half-finished before you write a single paragraph.

Edit in Separate Passes

Do not try to fix everything at once. The ADHD brain is prone to getting lost in details. The dyslexic brain struggles with spelling, grammar, and word retrieval. Trying to edit for ideas, structure, clarity, and mechanics simultaneously is overwhelming and ineffective.

Do a content edit first. Read the entire draft and ask only whether the argument makes sense. Are the ideas in the right order? Is there anything missing? Does the conclusion follow from the evidence? Do not fix commas. Do not correct spelling. Focus only on what you are saying. Once the content is solid, do a clarity edit. Read each sentence and ask whether it says what you intended. Simplify sentences that are tangled. Clarify sentences that are vague. Finally, do a mechanics edit. This is where you fix spelling, grammar, and punctuation. Run spellcheck. Use a grammar tool like Grammarly. Read the essay backward to catch missing words and repeated phrases.

Separating the editing passes prevents you from perfecting a sentence in a paragraph that you later cut entirely. It reduces the cognitive load of each editing session. And it gives you permission to write a messy first draft, because you know you will clean it up in dedicated passes later.

The Bottom Line

Writing essays with ADHD and dyslexia is not about forcing your brain to follow a standard process. It is about designing a process that fits your brain. Separate thinking from writing by speaking your ideas first. Use dictation to draft at the speed of speech. Build a detailed scaffold before you write so you never face a blank page. Edit in separate passes for content, clarity, and mechanics. Your brain outpaces your fingers for a reason. It has ideas worth sharing. Build a workflow that lets them out.
